questions.question:
Suppose there are k sorted lists of n/k elements each. The time complexity of producing a sorted list of all these elements is: (Hint: Use a heap data structure)
A. O(n log(n/k))
B. Theta(n log k)
C. Omega(n log n)
D. Omega(n^(k/2))
questions.to_find: Time complexity of merging k sorted lists each of size n/k using a heap

understand.plain: We have k sorted lists, each containing n/k elements. We want to merge all of them into one sorted list. Using a min-heap of size k, we always extract the minimum from the heap and insert the next element from the corresponding list. The total number of elements is n, and each heap operation takes O(log k) time.

keywords.explain: Merging k sorted sequences simultaneously into one sorted output
keywords.example: Merging 4 sorted arrays of 25 elements each into 1 sorted array of 100 elements

keywords.explain: A priority queue that always gives the smallest element; insert and extract-min both take O(log(heap_size)) time
keywords.example: A heap of size k stores one element per list (the current minimum of each list)

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='90' height='40' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='55' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>List 1</text><rect x='110' y='10' width='90' height='40' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='155' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>List 2</text><text x='215' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>...</text><rect x='230' y='10' width='90' height='40' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='275' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>List k</text><rect x='130' y='80' width='120' height='44' class='hi' rx='4'/><text x='190' y='107' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Min-Heap (k)</text><line x1='55' y1='50' x2='160' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='155' y1='50' x2='175' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='275' y1='50' x2='220' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='130' y='136' width='120' height='18' class='bg' rx='3'/><text x='190' y='149' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Sorted Output (n total)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: k sorted input lists feed into a min-heap of size k; the heap produces a single sorted output of n elements

given.aim: Find the time complexity of merging k sorted lists of n/k elements each into one sorted list using a heap

terms.meaning: Number of sorted input lists
terms.example: k = 4 lists
terms.connects: Determines heap size and therefore per-element cost O(log k)

terms.meaning: Number of elements in each list
terms.example: If n=100 and k=4, each list has 25 elements
terms.connects: Total elements across all lists = k * (n/k) = n

terms.meaning: Each insert or extract-min on a heap of size k costs O(log k)
terms.example: Extracting the minimum from a heap of 4 elements takes O(log 4) = O(2) steps
terms.connects: We do one heap operation per element, so total cost = n * O(log k)

given.plan: Maintain a min-heap of k elements (one from each list). For each of the n total elements, extract the minimum (O(log k)) and insert the next element from the same list (O(log k)). Total: Theta(n log k).

steps.title: Build initial heap
steps.apply: Insert the first element from each of the k lists into the min-heap. Building the heap takes O(k) time (using heapify).
steps.note: O(k) <= O(n) so this does not dominate the total

steps.title: Repeatedly extract and insert
steps.formula_raw: n * O(log k) = O(n log k)
steps.apply: For each of the n total elements: extract-min from heap (O(log k)), add it to output, then insert the next element from the same list into the heap (O(log k)). Total operations = n, each costing O(log k). Total = O(n log k).
steps.note: Since every element must be processed, Omega(n log k) also holds, giving Theta(n log k)

questions.formula_note: k-way merge using a min-heap: n elements each requiring O(log k) heap operations gives Theta(n log k) total time

questions.question:
Which one of the following in-place sorting algorithms needs the minimum number of swaps?
A. Quick sort
B. Insertion sort
C. Selection sort
D. Heap sort
questions.to_find: Which in-place sorting algorithm requires the minimum number of swaps

understand.plain: We compare Quick sort, Insertion sort, Selection sort, and Heap sort on the number of swaps each performs. Selection sort is unique: in each pass it finds the minimum element and does exactly ONE swap to place it in position, giving at most n-1 swaps total.

keywords.explain: An exchange of two elements in the array
keywords.example: Swapping elements at positions 0 and 3 in [4,2,1,3] gives [3,2,1,4]

keywords.explain: A sort that uses O(1) extra space beyond the input array
keywords.example: Selection sort, Insertion sort, Heap sort are all in-place

understand.visual_alt: Comparison table: Selection sort does at most n-1 swaps (one per pass), while other algorithms may do O(n^2) swaps in the worst case

given.aim: Identify which in-place algorithm minimises the number of swaps

terms.meaning: In each of the n-1 passes, exactly one swap places the minimum into its correct position
terms.example: For n=5, at most 4 swaps regardless of input
terms.connects: Upper bound is O(n) swaps, which is the tightest among all listed algorithms

terms.meaning: May shift (equivalent to swap) O(n) times per element in the worst case
terms.example: Reverse-sorted array: O(n^2) swaps total
terms.connects: Much worse than selection sort in the worst case

terms.meaning: Quick sort can do O(n^2) swaps; Heap sort does O(n log n) swaps
terms.example: Heap sort swaps during heapify and extract-max phases
terms.connects: Both exceed selection sort's O(n) swap count

given.plan: Selection sort makes exactly one swap per pass (to place the minimum), giving Theta(n) swaps worst case — the minimum among all the listed algorithms.

steps.title: Analyse Selection sort
steps.apply: In pass i, scan positions i..n-1 to find the minimum, then swap it to position i. One swap per pass, n-1 passes total => at most n-1 swaps.
steps.note: If the minimum is already in place, no swap is needed, so could be fewer

steps.title: Compare with other algorithms
steps.apply: Insertion sort: O(n^2) swaps (reverse-sorted input). Quick sort: O(n^2) swaps (worst case). Heap sort: O(n log n) swaps. Selection sort: O(n) swaps. Minimum is Selection sort.
steps.note: The question asks for minimum swaps, not minimum comparisons

solution.result: Selection sort — at most n-1 swaps (O(n))

questions.formula_note: Selection sort performs at most n-1 swaps (one per pass), giving the fewest swaps among common in-place sorting algorithms

questions.question:
Which of the following sorting algorithms has the lowest worst-case complexity?
A. Merge sort
B. Bubble sort
C. Quick sort
D. Selection sort
questions.to_find: Which sorting algorithm has the best (lowest) worst-case time complexity

understand.plain: We compare the worst-case time complexities of four sorting algorithms. Merge sort always runs in Theta(n log n) in the worst case. Quick sort can degrade to O(n^2). Bubble sort and Selection sort are always O(n^2). So Merge sort has the lowest worst-case complexity.

keywords.explain: The maximum time an algorithm takes over all possible inputs of size n
keywords.example: Quick sort's worst case is O(n^2) when the pivot is always the smallest or largest element

keywords.explain: A divide-and-conquer sort that always splits evenly and merges in O(n) time per level
keywords.example: Array of 8 elements: 3 levels of splitting, each level O(n) merge = O(n log n) total

understand.visual_alt: Worst-case complexities: Merge sort O(n log n), Quick sort O(n^2), Bubble sort O(n^2), Selection sort O(n^2)

given.aim: Find the algorithm with the smallest worst-case time complexity

terms.meaning: Always Theta(n log n) — the split is always balanced
terms.example: Even reverse-sorted input takes Theta(n log n)
terms.connects: Best worst-case among the four options

terms.meaning: O(n^2) when the chosen pivot is always the extreme element
terms.example: Sorted array with first element as pivot
terms.connects: Much worse than merge sort in the worst case

terms.meaning: Always Theta(n^2) regardless of input
terms.example: Both require O(n^2) comparisons even for random input
terms.connects: Worst among the options listed

given.plan: Compare the known worst-case complexities: Merge sort = Theta(n log n), Quick sort = O(n^2), Bubble sort = O(n^2), Selection sort = O(n^2). Merge sort wins.

steps.title: List worst-case complexities
steps.apply: Merge sort: Theta(n log n). Quick sort: Theta(n^2). Bubble sort: Theta(n^2). Selection sort: Theta(n^2).
steps.note: Merge sort's worst-case matches its best-case and average-case — all Theta(n log n)

steps.title: Identify minimum
steps.formula_raw: n log n << n^2 for large n
steps.apply: n log n grows much slower than n^2. Merge sort at Theta(n log n) is strictly better worst-case than all three others at Theta(n^2).
steps.note: Heap sort also has Theta(n log n) worst case but is not listed as an option

solution.result: Merge sort with worst-case complexity Theta(n log n)

questions.formula_note: Merge sort guarantees Theta(n log n) worst case via the recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + Theta(n), solved by the Master theorem

questions.question:
What is the number of swaps required to sort n elements using selection sort, in the worst case?
A. Theta(n)
B. Theta(n log n)
C. Theta(n^2)
D. Theta(n^2 log n)
questions.to_find: Worst-case number of swaps in selection sort for n elements

understand.plain: Selection sort works by finding the minimum unsorted element and swapping it into position. In each of the n-1 passes, it performs exactly one swap (or zero if already in place). So the total number of swaps is at most n-1 = Theta(n), regardless of the input.

keywords.explain: Scan the unsorted portion to find the minimum, then swap it to the front of the unsorted portion
keywords.example: Pass 1 of [3,1,4,2]: find min=1 at index 1, swap with index 0 => [1,3,4,2]

keywords.explain: Maximum possible swaps over all n-element inputs
keywords.example: For n=5: at most 4 swaps, e.g., [5,4,3,2,1] requires 4 swaps

understand.visual_alt: Selection sort makes one swap per pass: n-1 passes means at most n-1 = Theta(n) swaps

given.aim: Determine the worst-case number of swaps in selection sort

terms.meaning: Selection sort has exactly n-1 passes to place n-1 elements
terms.example: For n=4 elements, 3 passes suffice
terms.connects: Bounds the total number of swaps to n-1

terms.meaning: Each pass does at most one swap (place minimum of unsorted portion)
terms.example: Pass i places the i-th smallest element with one swap
terms.connects: Total swaps = at most n-1, which is Theta(n)

terms.meaning: Worst case is when every element is out of place, requiring a swap every pass
terms.example: [n, n-1, ..., 2, 1] requires exactly n-1 swaps
terms.connects: Confirms the bound is tight: Theta(n) not O(n)

given.plan: Each of the n-1 passes does one swap. Maximum swaps = n-1. This is Theta(n).

steps.title: Count swaps per pass
steps.apply: In pass i (i = 0 to n-2): find minimum in positions i..n-1 and swap it to position i. This is exactly one swap (or zero if already in position).
steps.note: Unlike insertion sort, selection sort never does multiple swaps in a single pass

steps.title: Compute total worst-case swaps
steps.apply: With n-1 passes each doing at most 1 swap, total swaps <= n-1. This is achieved by a reverse-sorted array. Hence worst case = Theta(n).
steps.note: Compare with insertion sort (Theta(n^2) swaps) and heap sort (Theta(n log n) swaps)

solution.result: Theta(n) swaps in the worst case

questions.formula_note: Selection sort's key property: exactly one swap per pass, giving at most n-1 swaps total regardless of input order

questions.question:
The number of elements that can be sorted in Theta(log n) time using heap sort is
A. Theta(1)
B. Theta(sqrt(log n))
C. Theta(log n / log(log n))
D. Theta(log n)
questions.to_find: How many elements m can be sorted in Theta(log n) time using heap sort

understand.plain: Heap sort on m elements takes Theta(m log m) time. We need Theta(m log m) = Theta(log n). So we must solve for m in terms of n. Setting m log m = log n and solving gives m = Theta(log n / log(log n)).

keywords.explain: Sorting m elements with heap sort takes Theta(m log m) time

keywords.term: solving m log m = log n
keywords.explain: We substitute m = log n / log(log n) and verify it satisfies the equation
keywords.example: If n = 2^16, log n = 16, log(log n) = 4, so m = 4; m log m = 4*2 = 8 ~ 16 (correct order)

understand.visual_alt: We need m log m = Theta(log n). Substituting m = log n / log(log n) satisfies this equation asymptotically.

given.aim: Find m such that heap sort on m elements runs in Theta(log n) time

terms.meaning: Theta(m log m) for m elements
terms.example: m=8: 8*3=24 operations
terms.connects: Set equal to log n to find m

terms.term: equation m log m = log n
terms.meaning: The constraint on m given the time budget Theta(log n)
terms.example: If log n = 8, we need m where m log m = 8; m~3 since 3*1.58~5 or m~4 since 4*2=8
terms.connects: Solving this gives m = Theta(log n / log(log n))

terms.meaning: Check that m = log n / log(log n) satisfies m log m = Theta(log n)
terms.example: log(m) = log(log n / log(log n)) = log(log n) - log(log(log n)) = Theta(log(log n))
terms.connects: m * log(m) = (log n / log(log n)) * Theta(log(log n)) = Theta(log n) -- confirmed

given.plan: Set Theta(m log m) = Theta(log n). Let m = c * log n / log(log n). Compute log m = log(log n) - log(log(log n)) = Theta(log(log n)). Then m log m = Theta(log n / log(log n)) * Theta(log(log n)) = Theta(log n). Confirmed.

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.apply: Heap sort on m elements costs Theta(m log m). We need this equal to Theta(log n). So solve: m log m = Theta(log n).
steps.note: This is an implicit equation in m; we test each answer option

steps.title: Test option C: m = Theta(log n / log(log n))
steps.apply: If m = log n / log(log n), then log m = log(log n) - log(log(log n)) = Theta(log(log n)). Therefore m log m = (log n / log(log n)) * Theta(log(log n)) = Theta(log n). This matches the required budget.
steps.note: Option D (m = log n) gives m log m = log n * log(log n) which is bigger than log n -- too slow

solution.result: Theta(log n / log(log n)) elements can be sorted in Theta(log n) time using heap sort

questions.formula_note: Setting heap sort's Theta(m log m) equal to the time budget Theta(log n) and solving asymptotically gives m = Theta(log n / log(log n))

questions.question:
Which one of the following is the tightest upper bound that represents the number of swaps required to sort n numbers using selection sort?
A. O(log n)
B. O(n)
C. O(n log n)
D. O(n^2)
questions.to_find: Tightest upper bound on number of swaps in selection sort for n numbers

understand.plain: Selection sort always does at most one swap per pass. With n-1 passes, the maximum number of swaps is n-1, which is O(n). Since no other listed bound is tighter than O(n) while still being correct, O(n) is the tightest valid upper bound.

keywords.explain: The smallest function class that still correctly bounds the quantity from above
keywords.example: For a quantity that is always <= 10n, O(n) is tighter than O(n^2) even though both are valid upper bounds

keywords.explain: At most one swap per pass, n-1 passes, so at most n-1 swaps total

understand.visual_alt: Selection sort: n-1 passes, each with at most 1 swap => at most n-1 swaps total => O(n) upper bound

given.aim: Find the tightest (smallest) correct upper bound for selection sort swap count

terms.meaning: Selection sort iterates n-1 times to place all elements
terms.example: For n=6, 5 passes needed
terms.connects: Directly limits total swaps to n-1

terms.meaning: Each pass swaps the minimum to its correct position
terms.example: Pass 2: swap element at position 2 with the minimum of elements 2..n-1
terms.connects: Combined with n-1 passes: total <= n-1 swaps = O(n)

terms.meaning: O(log n) would be too small — consider n=8: n-1=7 swaps but log(8)=3
terms.example: Reverse-sorted array of 8 needs 7 swaps, exceeding log(8)=3
terms.connects: Rules out option A; O(n) is the correct tightest bound

given.plan: Selection sort: at most 1 swap per pass, n-1 passes, so at most n-1 = O(n) swaps. O(log n) is too small (fails for reverse-sorted input). O(n) is the tightest valid upper bound.

steps.title: Count maximum swaps
steps.apply: Each of the n-1 passes does exactly one swap in the worst case (when the minimum is never already in place). Maximum swaps = n-1.
steps.note: This is achieved by a reverse-sorted array

steps.title: Identify tightest upper bound
steps.apply: n-1 swaps = O(n). Among the options A=O(log n), B=O(n), C=O(n log n), D=O(n^2): O(log n) is too small, O(n) correctly upper-bounds n-1, and O(n log n)/O(n^2) are valid but looser. Tightest is O(n).
steps.note: The tightest means we want the smallest class that still holds

solution.result: O(n) — selection sort requires at most n-1 swaps

questions.formula_note: Selection sort performs at most n-1 swaps (one per pass), giving O(n) as the tightest upper bound

questions.question: The minimum number of comparisons required to find the minimum and the maximum of 100 numbers is ________
questions.to_find: Minimum number of comparisons to simultaneously find both minimum and maximum of 100 numbers

understand.plain: Finding just the minimum takes n-1 comparisons, and finding just the maximum also takes n-1 comparisons, totalling 2n-2. But by processing elements in pairs, we can do better. Compare each pair first (1 comparison), then compare the pair's smaller with the current minimum (1 comparison) and the pair's larger with the current maximum (1 comparison). This uses 3 comparisons per 2 elements, giving 3n/2 - 2 total for even n.

keywords.explain: Compare elements in pairs: 1 comparison to split each pair into a candidate-min and candidate-max, then 2 more comparisons to update global min/max
keywords.example: For pair (5, 3): 1 comparison (3 < 5), then compare 3 with global-min, compare 5 with global-max

keywords.explain: Instead of 4 comparisons (2 elements x 2 comparisons each), we do 3 per pair, saving 1 per pair
keywords.example: For 100 elements = 50 pairs: 50 * 3 = 150, minus 2 for initialization = 148; the exact formula for n=100 gives 147

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='60' height='30' class='bg' rx='3'/><text x='40' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>a</text><rect x='80' y='10' width='60' height='30' class='bg' rx='3'/><text x='110' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>b</text><text x='150' y='28' class='sub mut'>compare</text><text x='150' y='44' class='sub mut'>(1 cmp)</text><rect x='200' y='10' width='70' height='30' class='hi' rx='3'/><text x='235' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>smaller</text><rect x='280' y='10' width='70' height='30' class='hi' rx='3'/><text x='315' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>larger</text><line x1='200' y1='60' x2='200' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='280' y1='60' x2='280' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='140' y='80' width='90' height='30' class='ok' rx='3'/><text x='185' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink' style='fill:#fff'>vs global min</text><rect x='240' y='80' width='90' height='30' class='no' rx='3'/><text x='285' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink' style='fill:#fff'>vs global max</text><text x='210' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>3 comparisons per pair of elements</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Pair (a, b): 1 comparison to find smaller and larger, then 1 comparison of smaller vs global-min, 1 comparison of larger vs global-max = 3 comparisons per pair

given.aim: Find the minimum number of comparisons to find both min and max of n=100 numbers

terms.meaning: Even number of elements
terms.example: 50 pairs can be formed
terms.connects: Formula: 3*floor(n/2) - 2 + (n mod 2 == 1 ? 2 : 0) for odd n; for even n: 3n/2 - 2

terms.meaning: Compare the first two elements to initialize min and max (1 comparison), then for each subsequent pair compare them together (1) and each against min/max (2 more = 3 per pair)
terms.example: Init: compare a[0] and a[1] => 1 comparison, set min and max. Then process a[2],a[3] as a pair => 3 comparisons. And so on.
terms.connects: Total = 1 (init) + 3 * 49 (remaining 49 pairs) = 1 + 147 = 148. Re-check: formula gives 3*(100/2) - 2 = 150 - 2 = 148.

terms.meaning: For even n: min comparisons = 3n/2 - 2
terms.example: n=4: 3*2-2=4. Verify: init with pair1 (1 cmp), then pair2 (3 cmps) = 4. Correct.
terms.connects: For n=100: 3*50-2 = 148. But GATE answer is 147, from initialization trick.

given.plan: Use the tournament (pair-wise) algorithm. Initialize min and max from the first two elements (1 comparison). For each of the remaining 49 pairs, do 3 comparisons. Total = 1 + 49*3 = 1 + 147 = 148. However, the standard GATE answer for this problem is 148. Let me verify: standard formula 3*floor(n/2) - 2 for even n = 3*50-2 = 148.

steps.title: Naive approach (for comparison)
steps.apply: Find minimum: n-1 comparisons. Find maximum separately: n-1 comparisons. Total = 2(n-1) = 2*99 = 198 comparisons.
steps.note: This is not optimal; the tournament method does better

steps.title: Tournament (pair-wise) algorithm
steps.apply:
Step 1: Compare first two elements a[0] and a[1] (1 comparison). Set min = smaller, max = larger.
For i = 2, 4, 6, ..., 98 (49 pairs):
  Compare a[i] with a[i+1] (1 comparison)
  Compare smaller of pair with current min (1 comparison)
  Compare larger of pair with current max (1 comparison)
Total comparisons = 1 + 49*3 = 1 + 147 = 148.
Formula check: 3*(100/2) - 2 = 150 - 2 = 148.
steps.note: This is the information-theoretically optimal algorithm for finding both min and max simultaneously; the GATE 2014 Set 1 official answer is 148

solution.result: 148 comparisons (using the pair-wise tournament algorithm: 3*floor(n/2) - 2 for even n=100)

questions.formula_note: Simultaneous min-max using pair comparisons: 3*floor(n/2) - 2 comparisons for even n, versus naive 2(n-1); for n=100 this gives 148
